learning.summary: PXE allows computers to boot from the network to install operating systems without local installation media.
learning.explanation:
PXE (Preboot Execution Environment) is a firmware-supported network boot technology used to start an operating system installer from a network. The client must support network boot and have it enabled in the firmware boot order.

A typical PXE deployment uses DHCP to provide network configuration and identify boot services, while TFTP transfers the initial boot files. PXE is commonly used to deploy operating systems across many computers efficiently.

facts.text: PXE stands for Preboot Execution Environment.
PXE requires firmware support for network boot.
DHCP provides network configuration during PXE boot.
TFTP commonly delivers the initial boot files.

examples.text: A school deploys Windows to an entire computer lab by booting each PC from a PXE server.

examTips: Know PXE purpose and DHCP/TFTP relationship.
commonMistakes: Assuming PXE works without firmware support.
scenarios: Deploy OS to many computers over a LAN.
pbqIdeas: Configure firmware and identify PXE dependencies.

reviewNotes: DHCP/TFTP details are enriched beyond explicit transcript evidence.
Merged remote-network-installation guidance per discovery review.
